Gene_Accession_Number.reason: AT4G30530 is explicitly given in Experimental Procedures for GGP1 ORF/CDS and mutant transcript analysis.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The title and summary explicitly identify GGP1 as γ-glutamyl peptidase 1.

Source_Species.reason: The study uses Arabidopsis/A. thaliana genes throughout.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly stated throughout the paper.

Applied_Species.reason: Mutants and expression analyses were performed in Arabidopsis plants.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the plant species used for in planta validation.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The corresponding wild type is explicitly stated as Columbia-0 (Col-0).

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: Results and summary support roles in glutathione degradation and in glucosinolate/camalexin biosynthesis via GSH-conjugate processing.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper proposes GGP1 functions in cytosolic GSH degradation in primary sulfur metabolism and also in secondary sulfur metabolism through GSH-conjugate processing for glucosinolate/camalexin synthesis.

Primary_Substrate.reason: GSH is directly tested as substrate; GSH conjugates in glucosinolate/camalexin synthesis are discussed based on prior identification and current interpretation.

Primary_Product.reason: The yeast and enzyme assay sections state GSH is degraded into Glu and Cys-Gly, and Discussion states GGPs directly produce Glu without ATP consumption.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper shows GGP1 degrades GSH and discusses its known role in processing GSH conjugates in glucosinolate and camalexin biosynthesis.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Discussion explicitly states GGP1 belongs to peptidase family C26 (γ-glutamyl hydrolase family) in MEROPS.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Discussion and model refer to cytosolic GSH degradation by GGP1.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: GGP1 is presented as a downstream glutathione degradation enzyme and also involved at the shared GSH-conjugate processing point for secondary sulfur metabolism.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: GGP1 directly degrades glutathione, and metabolomics support effects on glucosinolates/isothiocyanates with discussion linking it to camalexin synthesis.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper proposes GGP1 is the main GSH degradation route in mature organs under sulfur-sufficient conditions, but does not explicitly call it rate-limiting or a control step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Glutathione, glucosinolates, isothiocyanates, and camalexin are all explicitly discussed as metabolites affected or connected to GGP1.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These classes are directly inferable from the named metabolites: glutathione is a thiol, glucosinolates are glucosinolates, isothiocyanates are isothiocyanates, and camalexin is a phytoalexin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Summary states glutathione is a major sulfur repository; Discussion states glucosinolates/isothiocyanates and camalexin function in defense.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show ggp1 mutants accumulate more GSH under normal, sulfur-deficient, and nitrogen-deficient conditions; OAS rises under sulfur deficiency; several glucosinolates and ITCs decrease.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state GGP1 transcripts were abundant in rosette leaves, showed a tendency to be higher in mature than young leaves, and were among abundant GSH-degradation-related transcripts.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Figure 2 and Figure 5b support high expression in rosette leaves and 2-week-old liquid-cultured plants; Results state GGP1 is slightly upregulated under nitrogen deficiency.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These conditions/treatments were explicitly used: sulfur deficiency, nitrogen deficiency, and BSO treatment.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are explicitly used in Results for GGP1: yeast complementation, recombinant enzyme assays/kinetics, T-DNA mutants, BSO double mutant analysis, qRT-PCR, and metabolomics.

Omics_Data.reason: Widely targeted metabolomics by LC-QqQ-MS/MS is explicitly described, and no accession number is reported.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a plausible implication, but the paper does not explicitly discuss breeding or engineering applications.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show reduced glucosinolate and isothiocyanate levels in ggp1 mutants, and the dual role is explicitly discussed, implying tradeoffs upon manipulation.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is supported by Results: yeast complementation, in vitro specificity, GSH accumulation in mutants, elevated OAS under sulfur deficiency, and decreased glucosinolates/isothiocyanates.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion explicitly states the GGP route is more energy efficient than GGCT because it directly generates Glu without ATP-dependent 5-oxoproline conversion, and structural modeling of GGP1 with GSH conjugate is presented.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: AT4G30550 is explicitly listed in the E. coli expression strain construction section.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The gene is explicitly referred to as GGP3, a γ-glutamyl peptidase family member.

Applied_Species.reason: Expression profiling was performed in Arabidopsis plants, though no ggp3 mutant was tested.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: Glutathione degradation by GGP3 is directly supported biochemically, but direct involvement of GGP3 in glucosinolate/camalexin biosynthesis is not experimentally tested here and is mostly inferred from prior work/family analogy.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper proposes GGP3 likely contributes to cytosolic GSH degradation alongside GGP1, i.e., primary sulfur metabolism as an auxiliary/redundant route.

Primary_Substrate.reason: GSH was directly used as substrate in recombinant enzyme assays for GGP3.

Primary_Product.reason: The assay quantified released Cys-Gly from GSH degradation; by pathway logic and comparison with GGP1 discussion, Glu is produced as the other product.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Hydrolysis of GSH in vitro is supported, but 'likely also processes GSH conjugates by analogy to GGP1' is not directly demonstrated in this paper for GGP3.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Discussion places GGP1 in peptidase family C26 and compares conserved residues/homology with GGP3, supporting GGP3 as the same family member.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper explicitly refers to cytosolic GSH degradation in general, but does not directly state GGP3 subcellular localization.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: GGP3 is analyzed as a GSH degradation enzyme, placing it in the downstream degradation step of glutathione.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: GGP3 directly degrades glutathione in vitro.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not establish GGP3 as rate-limiting, key control, or redundantly validated by mutant phenotype; its role remains proposed/likely.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Glutathione is the directly tested metabolite for GGP3.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states glutathione is a major sulfur repository/storage metabolite.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results support that recombinant GGP3 degrades GSH and the paper proposes contribution with GGP1 to cytosolic GSH degradation, though no mutant phenotype is shown.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state GGP3 is constitutively expressed and significantly higher in mature than young leaves.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results show expression across tissues, slight upregulation under nitrogen deficiency in wild type, and increased GGP3 expression in the ggp1 mutant under sulfur deficiency.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Sulfur deficiency and nitrogen deficiency were explicitly analyzed for GGP3 expression.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Recombinant protein enzyme assay/kinetics and qRT-PCR expression profiling are directly used; compensatory expression in mutants is inferred from expression data.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not discuss breeding/engineering applications for GGP3 explicitly.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Dual-purpose effects for GGP3 are plausible from family context, but not directly demonstrated here for GGP3.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The three statements match the paper: in vitro GSH degradation with notable catalytic efficiency, constitutive/higher mature-organ expression, and proposal that GGP3 likely assists GGP1 and may compensate under sulfur deficiency.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states no direct ggp3 mutant phenotype here; its role is inferred from biochemical activity/expression and structural comparison.

corrections.reason: The paper does not establish GGP3 as rate-limiting, key control, or redundantly validated by mutant phenotype; its role remains proposed/likely.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The gene is explicitly named γ-glutamyl cyclotransferase 2;1.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly the source species.

Applied_Species.reason: In planta mutant and expression analyses were performed in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the applied species for validation.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: GGCT2;1 is analyzed throughout as a glutathione degradation enzyme.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper explicitly contrasts the GGCT-mediated pathway with the GGP-mediated GSH degradation pathway.

Primary_Substrate.reason: GGCT2;1 complements yeast GSH-utilization mutants and is discussed specifically as acting on GSH.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper states the GGCT pathway produces 5-oxoproline, and by known pathway context in the paper GSH degradation yields Cys-Gly plus 5-oxoproline/Glu route.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Discussion explicitly states GGCTs produce 5-oxoproline from GSH degradation, contrasting with GGPs that directly produce Glu.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: As the enzyme name itself indicates, GGCT2;1 is a γ-glutamyl cyclotransferase; the paper consistently uses this class designation.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Discussion/model describe cytosolic GSH degradation involving GGCT2;1 under sulfur deficiency.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: GGCT2;1 acts in the downstream degradation step of glutathione.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: It directly degrades glutathione and is specifically implicated under sulfur deficiency.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports GGCT2;1 as an important sulfur-deficiency-responsive contributor, but does not explicitly define it as a rate-limiting or control step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Glutathione is the directly relevant metabolite measured in ggct2;1 mutants.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states glutathione is a major sulfur repository/storage metabolite.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Figure 3a shows ggct2;1 mutants have elevated GSH under sulfur deficiency but not under normal or nitrogen-deficient conditions.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results state GGCT2;1 expression is much lower than the other genes in all organs under normal conditions, and Figure 5b/Results report a 31-fold increase under sulfur deficiency.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Low constitutive expression, strong induction by sulfur deficiency, and increased GGCT2;1 expression after BSO in the ggp1 mutant are all stated in Results.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Sulfur deficiency and BSO treatment were explicitly examined for GGCT2;1.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Yeast complementation, knockout mutant GSH measurements, double-mutant BSO analysis, and qRT-PCR are all explicitly reported.

Omics_Data.reason: Widely targeted metabolomics by LC-QqQ-MS/MS is described for ggct2;1 and no accession is reported.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This application-oriented claim is not explicitly made in the paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper references prior work on root architecture under sulfur deficiency, but does not directly demonstrate tradeoffs from engineering GGCT2;1 in this study.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summary points are directly supported by Results and cited prior context in the Results/Discussion.
